moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the English Opening, a flexible and strategically rich alternative to 1.e4. By advancing the c-pawn, we control the central d5 square from afar and prepare to build a strong position without committing to a classical pawn center immediately. This move emphasizes piece play and maneuvering over early pawn tension, giving us excellent practical winning chances at the intermediate level. The English is particularly useful because it sidesteps well-prepared defenses to 1.e4 while keeping Black guessing about our setup.

Black responds with the Anglo-Dutch Defense, staking a claim on the long diagonal (a1-h8) and preparing to support central play with the e-pawn next. This move is aggressive and thematic—Black wants to establish a pawn presence on e4 and control central squares—but it also weakens the kingside and the e6 square, which you should keep in mind for future knight jumps or tactical ideas. You'll want to develop naturally (pieces like Nf3 and g3 fit well) and potentially exploit these weaknesses later if Black overextends.
